intro_script: Welcome back to Robotics Update for June 22, 2026. Today we cover AI infrastructure risks, breakthrough talks on dexterity, and advances in nanoscale touch sensing shaping the future of automation. Let's dive into five key stories.
outro_script: And that's your scoop for June 22, 2026! Remember those AI risks? Keep questioning the code in your day-to-day. Thanks for joining us; keep building a smarter future! Bye for now!

items.title: Opinion: The AI infrastructure risk robotics leaders can’t afford to ignore
items.summary: The robotics sector is undergoing a period of significant advancement, marked by increasingly sophisticated autonomous mobile robots. Industrial robots are becoming notably smarter and more adaptable in their operations. AI-powered vision systems are currently transforming key sectors such as manufacturing, logistics, and defense. These technological leaps fundamentally rely on underlying Artificial Intelligence infrastructure. Industry leaders must pay close attention to the associated risks within this rapidly evolving AI infrastructure.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Opinion: The AI infrastructure risk robotics leaders can’t afford to ignore." This report details that the advancements in the robotics sector are fundamentally tied to underlying Artificial Intelligence infrastructure and suggests industry focus on associated risks. Current developments indicate a period of significant advancement characterized by increasingly sophisticated autonomous mobile robots, while industrial robots are demonstrating greater operational adaptability. Specifically, AI-powered vision systems are transforming key sectors including manufacturing, logistics, and defense through these technological leaps. Industry leaders must direct attention toward the inherent risks within this rapidly evolving AI foundation. The necessity for robust oversight of foundational AI layers remains a central consideration for the technology sector going forward.

items.title: Interview with Sharpa’s Alicia Veneziani: ‘Dexterous manipulation is the key to useful humanoid robots’
items.summary: Locomotion capabilities, such as walking and running, are garnering significant attention in humanoid robotics research. However, many experts suggest that advanced movement is only a partial hurdle. The greater challenge for creating truly useful humanoid robots involves more familiar human abilities. Dexterous manipulation skills are identified by some researchers as the key area needing major advancement. This suggests fine motor control over mobility may be the current bottleneck in field utility.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Sharpa’s Alicia Veneziani: ‘Dexterous manipulation is the key to useful humanoid robots’." The report outlines that while locomotion abilities are receiving considerable focus in humanoid robotics research, experts point toward fine motor control as the more critical area for development. Specific discussion highlighted that advanced movement capabilities represent only one component of a functional bipedal machine. Instead, researchers identify dexterity in manipulation tasks as presenting the primary hurdle to real-world utility in humanoid systems. This suggests that achieving high levels of fine motor precision may currently impede the deployment of these robots in diverse field settings. Future industry efforts are expected to concentrate significant resources on advancing sophisticated grasping and object interaction protocols.

items.title: Interview with Digid’s Nils Könne and Christian Kreil: Nanoscale sensors could help solve robotics’ tactile sensing challenge
items.summary: Effective operation of robots in physical environments requires sophisticated environmental understanding beyond just software or processing power. This necessity has spurred significant interest in advanced sensing technologies for robotics. Nanoscale sensors are emerging as a key solution to address the current challenge of tactile sensing in machines. According to Nils Könne and Christian Kreil, these nanoscale sensors show promise for improving robotic interaction with the world. Developing such advanced physical sensing capabilities is crucial for the advancement of practical robotics applications.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Digid’s Nils Könne and Christian Kreil: Nanoscale sensors could help solve robotics’ tactile sensing challenge." The report details how advanced nanoscale sensors represent a promising solution to the longstanding technical hurdle of improving robotic tactile perception. Researchers noted that for robots to operate effectively in physical settings, understanding environmental data beyond mere software processing is required. These nano-sensors offer enhanced capability by providing detailed physical feedback, according to Könne and Kreil's discussion. Developing these advanced physical sensing modalities is identified as a critical step toward enabling practical, real-world applications of robotics technology moving forward.

items.title: Interview with Clearpath Robotics co-founder Ryan Gariepy: ‘Most industries in Canada are under-automated’
items.summary: Despite a strong national reputation for robotics research through universities and startups, Canadian industry adoption lags in practice. Ryan Gariepy, a co-founder of Clearpath Robotics, notes that most industries across Canada remain under-automated. This suggests a gap between advanced technological capacity and real-world industrial implementation. The technology has shown potential application across sectors like manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, mining, and defense. Addressing this gap is key to translating research strengths into broader economic deployment.
items.audio_script: From Robotics & Automation News, "Interview with Clearpath Robotics co-founder Ryan Gariepy: ‘Most industries in Canada are under-automated’." The report highlights a notable disparity between Canada's advanced robotic research base and its actual industrial deployment levels across various sectors. Co-founder Ryan Gariepy indicated that most industries within the nation currently operate at levels of automation below their potential capacity. This assessment points to a significant chasm existing between sophisticated technological capabilities developed in academic settings and their incorporation into day-to-day operational workflows across manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, mining, and defense sectors. Addressing this gap is identified as crucial for converting established research strengths into widespread economic deployment.

items.title: Güdel to show grinding beyond stationary robots with vertical, horizontal motion at Automate 2026
items.summary: Güdel is expanding the capabilities of heavy-duty robotic grinding. They have incorporated two additional degrees of freedom into their TMV and TMF systems. These updates allow for grinding motions that extend beyond traditional stationary robot setups. This enhanced functionality enables more versatile industrial applications. Güdel plans to showcase these advanced grinding capabilities at Automate 2026.
items.audio_script: From The Robot Report, "Güdel to show grinding beyond stationary robots with vertical, horizontal motion at Automate 2026." This report details an expansion of heavy-duty robotic grinding capabilities moving past fixed setups. Güdel has integrated two additional degrees of freedom into its existing TMV and TMF systems through this development. These updates fundamentally change the possible scope for industrial grinding motions by enabling movement beyond traditional stationary constraints, thereby expanding versatility across various manufacturing applications. The company plans to present these advanced, mobile grinding demonstrations at the Automate 2026 exhibition. This advancement indicates a significant move toward greater kinematic flexibility in heavy-duty robotic process automation.
